task: I’m helping a friend figure out housing near MIT in Cambridge, so could you use a real browser to look for apartments that are roughly within a 20-minute commute to MIT and keep this organized for me? Start with Google to find solid rental sites that actually have Cambridge and nearby Boston-area listings, then use places like Apartments.com and any other major listing sources you find to search for either 1-bedroom apartments under $3,000 a month or 2-bedroom apartments under $5,000 a month for a roommate setup. As you find listings that seem to fit, open each actual listing page in its own tab so I can visually compare the photos, addresses, and details later, and only keep tabs open for listings that really match the bedroom and budget limits. For every listing you keep, verify the rent, bedroom count, and building name or address on the listing page itself, then use Google Maps to check the commute to MIT and keep only the ones that look to be about 20 minutes or less. After that, create a spreadsheet in CryptPad Sheets called MIT Apartment Options and record about 20 good options with the building or address, monthly rent, number of bedrooms, estimated commute time to MIT, and the direct listing link. Please make sure every row in the sheet corresponds to a listing tab that is still open on the actual apartment page, because I want to be able to click around and inspect them afterward. Once the sheet is filled out, add a short note summarizing which neighborhoods seem to have the most within-budget options so I can see where the best concentration of listings is.
grader_reasoning:
R1: success - The agent successfully used Google to identify relevant rental listing sources (Apartments.com and Zumper) and initiated a search for apartments in Cambridge, MA, near MIT, adhering to the specified budget and bedroom criteria. This is evidenced by the search queries and the subsequent navigation to rental listing pages.

R2: success - The agent successfully identified rental sources, searched for 1-bedroom apartments under $3,000 and 2-bedroom apartments under $5,000, and verified commute times to MIT. It opened multiple candidate listings in separate tabs and maintained them throughout the process. The final spreadsheet in CryptPad contains 20 verified options with the required details, and the agent confirmed these listings were kept open for the user to inspect. The rubric requirement for opening candidate listings in separate tabs and adhering to the specified constraints is satisfied.

R3: success - The agent successfully identified rental listings, verified their details (rent, bedroom count, address) by navigating to the actual listing pages, and confirmed the commute times to MIT using Google Maps. It then created a CryptPad sheet and populated it with 20 qualifying options, including the required information (building/address, rent, bedrooms, commute time, and link). The final screenshot confirms the spreadsheet is populated with the verified data.

R4: success - The agent successfully used Google Maps to check commute times for various apartment listings. The action history shows the agent repeatedly querying Google Maps for specific addresses and the MIT destination, and the final spreadsheet includes an "Estimated commute to MIT" column with values consistently at or under 20 minutes (e.g., "4 min walk", "11 min transit", "15 min walk"). The agent verified these times and filtered the list accordingly before populating the spreadsheet.

R5: success - The agent successfully created a CryptPad sheet titled "MIT Apartment Options". The sheet contains 20 rows of apartment listings, each including the building/address, monthly rent, number of bedrooms, estimated commute time to MIT, and a direct listing link. The agent also included a summary note at the bottom of the sheet as requested. The final screenshot confirms the data is populated and the title is correct.

R6: success - The agent successfully created a spreadsheet in CryptPad titled "MIT Apartment Options" containing 20 apartment listings. The agent's final action history confirms that it kept 20 actual listing tabs open (tabs 0-19) corresponding to the 20 rows in the spreadsheet. The screenshots show the spreadsheet populated with the data, and the agent's final summary confirms that these tabs were maintained to allow for visual cross-checking.

R7: success - The agent successfully created a spreadsheet in CryptPad titled "MIT Apartment Options" and populated it with 20 listings. Crucially, in row 23 of the spreadsheet, the agent included a note: "Best concentration: Central Square/MIT/Kendall/Cambridgeport has the most within-budget, short-commute options. Harvard Square/Mid-Cambridge ad..." This note explicitly summarizes which neighborhoods have the most qualifying within-budget listings, satisfying the requirement.
